findings.title: Permissionless market creation lets attackers register arbitrary redemption assets and exchange-rate providers

best_agent_match.title: Module creation trusts an attacker-controlled rate provider

findings.title: CorkHook.beforeSwap can be called directly with forged swap context

best_agent_match.title: Swap-hook entrypoint can be driven with spoofed sender and pool metadata

findings.title: Near-expiry HIYA manipulation can force newly rolled markets to initialize CT at a severely discounted price

best_agent_match.title: Reserve-asset redemption is not tightly bound to the correct CT/DS series

rejected_candidates.title: Reserve-asset redemption is not tightly bound to the correct CT/DS series
rejected_candidates.reason: Source shows `returnRaWithCtDs` always redeems the current `globalAssetIdx` CT/DS for the selected market id and burns that exact pair; the PoC's mixed ids are explained by the attacker creating a separate fake market, not by a standalone series-binding bug.

rejected_candidates.title: Raw token donations can manipulate accounting before swap and mint flows
rejected_candidates.reason: The available source and incident writeups support HIYA manipulation plus unauthenticated `beforeSwap` as the loss drivers; there is no concrete evidence here that unsolicited token transfers into the proxy are directly consumed as trusted pricing/accounting inputs.

rejected_candidates.title: Unlock/settle flow exposes transient proxy balances to attacker-controlled callbacks
rejected_candidates.reason: The callback abuse is already explained by the missing caller/context validation on `beforeSwap`; no distinct transient-balance accounting flaw is supported beyond that root cause.
